description:
You are a developer tidying up your workspace and you realise the project still does **not** have an SSH key-pair dedicated to deployment.  
Create that key-pair, record its fingerprints, and make it usable for the rest of the team.

When you are finished, the machine **must** look exactly like this:

1. A new RSA-4096 key-pair with **no pass-phrase** exists:
   • Private key  :  /home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa  
   • Public  key  :  /home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa.pub  

2. The private key file permissions are **600** and the public key permissions are **644**.

3. The public key has been **appended** (do not overwrite) to the project access list found at  
   /home/user/project/.access/authorized_devs  
   – If the folder /home/user/project/.access/ does not yet exist, create it.  
   – The resulting authorized_devs file must contain the full public-key line in OpenSSH format, ending with the comment “project_dev@example.com”.

4. A plain-text log file named /home/user/ssh_setup.log summarises what you did.  
   The log must contain **exactly five lines**, each beginning with the given keyword, followed by a colon, then the recorded value with no extra spaces before or after the value:

        KEY_PATH:/home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa
        PUBLIC_KEY_PATH:/home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa.pub
        MD5_FINGERPRINT:<the md5 fingerprint of the public key>
        SHA256_FINGERPRINT:<the sha256 fingerprint of the public key>
        TIMESTAMP:<UTC time in ISO-8601 format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ>

   • Compute the two fingerprints with `ssh-keygen -l`  
     – MD5 must be produced with “-E md5” and **must not** include the leading “MD5:” prefix that ssh-keygen prints.  
     – SHA256 must be produced with “-E sha256” and **must not** include the leading “SHA256:” prefix.  
   • The test harness will recalculate the fingerprints and compare them with the values in the log, so they must match exactly.  
   • The TIMESTAMP line should reflect the moment you created the log (use UTC).

5. Make sure the key comment is exactly: project_dev@example.com

Use as few commands as you like (2–3 commands is enough), but you are free to employ additional ones for checking your work.  
Do **not** embed passwords or pass-phrases anywhere; the key must be completely unencrypted.

Once you believe the system matches points 1-5, you are done.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful completion (the automated tests will verify these):

Directories that must exist:
  /home/user/.ssh    (already present in container images)
  /home/user/project/.access

Files that must exist with exact paths and permissions:
  /home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa          mode 600
  /home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa.pub      mode 644
  /home/user/project/.access/authorized_devs (permission irrelevant to test)

Log file:
  /home/user/ssh_setup.log  – must have exactly 5 non-blank lines, in the order and key names given in the public task description.

Fingerprints:
  The MD5 and SHA256 fingerprint strings written in the log file must be identical to those produced by:
        ssh-keygen -l -E md5    -f /home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa.pub
        ssh-keygen -l -E sha256 -f /home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa.pub
  and stripped of the “MD5:” / “SHA256:” prefixes.

Public-key presence:
  The literal line found in /home/user/.ssh/project_dev_rsa.pub must also appear, verbatim, as its own line in /home/user/project/.access/authorized_devs (the file may contain additional pre-existing lines).

Comment string:
  The final field (after the key material) inside both the .pub file and the authorized_devs file must be exactly: project_dev@example.com
name: task_000000_95fadd36
